title: Loops and branching

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java while, do-while, counted for, and enhanced-for loops repeat a body while preserving a progress argument, termination condition, and valid traversal state.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A counted loop visits indices 0 through length - 1, maintains a running total, and is tested for empty, one-element, and many-element arrays. Your goal is to implement and debug counted, sentinel and collection traversals. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: First source and main; pf state expressions; if and switch. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java while, do-while, counted for, and enhanced-for loops repeat a body while preserving a progress argument, termination condition, and valid traversal state. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Enhanced-for does not expose a writable index, mutation can invalidate iteration, off-by-one bounds are common, and sentinel loops must handle missing or malformed termination input. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Changing a local element variable inside an enhanced-for loop replaces the corresponding array or collection element.

includes: Use while, do, for and enhanced-for loops with deliberate termination and bounded access.
Implement and debug counted, sentinel and collection traversals.
excludes: Enhanced-for does not expose a writable index, mutation can invalidate iteration, off-by-one bounds are common, and sentinel loops must handle missing or malformed termination input.

outcomes: Implement and debug counted, sentinel and collection traversals.

examples.title: Loops and branching: direct language case
examples.situation: A counted loop visits indices 0 through length - 1, maintains a running total, and is tested for empty, one-element, and many-element arrays.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A counted loop visits indices 0 through length - 1, maintains a running total, and is tested for empty, one-element, and many-element arrays.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Select a loop whose state matches the job, prove progress toward termination, and use an index, iterator, or collection operation when the underlying structure must change.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Select a loop whose state matches the job, prove progress toward termination, and use an index, iterator, or collection operation when the underlying structure must change.

examples.title: Loops and branching: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A sentinel loop reads until a quit token or end of input and updates input on every path so malformed data cannot create an infinite loop.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A sentinel loop reads until a quit token or end of input and updates input on every path so malformed data cannot create an infinite loop.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Enhanced-for does not expose a writable index, mutation can invalidate iteration, off-by-one bounds are common, and sentinel loops must handle missing or malformed termination input.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Changing a local element variable inside an enhanced-for loop replaces the corresponding array or collection element.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and debug counted, sentinel and collection traversals.

misconceptions.correction: Select a loop whose state matches the job, prove progress toward termination, and use an index, iterator, or collection operation when the underlying structure must change.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Changing a local element variable inside an enhanced-for loop replaces the corresponding array or collection element.

analogy.example: a control panel whose typed gauges, expressions, switches, and repeating cycles produce observable state can introduce Loops and branching, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: tracing primitive values, operators, decisions, and loop progress
limitations: the panel does not model promotion, overflow, short-circuit evaluation, scope, or JVM representation exactly

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and debug counted, sentinel and collection traversals. Keep this boundary in view: Enhanced-for does not expose a writable index, mutation can invalidate iteration, off-by-one bounds are common, and sentinel loops must handle missing or malformed termination input.
